perturbation.original_text: This agreement is governed by Austrian law, to the exclusion of the UN Convention on the International Sale of Goods and the IPRG [law on in ternational private law]. Jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is governed by Austrian law, to the exclusion of the UN Convention on the International Sale of Goods and the IPRG [law on in ternational private law].
perturbation.explanation: By removing the jurisdiction clause and placing it elsewhere, the contract now lacks a clearly defined jurisdiction for dispute resolution, potentially conflicting with mandatory jurisdiction requirements for contracts under Austrian law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Austrian Law on Civil Procedure, jurisdiction clause requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian law requires clarity regarding jurisdiction in contracts to ensure enforceability. By deleting the jurisdiction clause, the enforceability of the contract under Austrian law becomes questionable as the location for resolving disputes is no longer explicitly defined, creating ambiguity that could be challenged in court. JN § 92 details the requirements for jurisdiction agreements, which are not fulfilled in this case. The removal makes it difficult for both parties to know in which court to file a claim.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: National Firearms Act | Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives Skip to main content You are here Home » What We Do » Rules and Regulations » Laws on Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives Español Rules and Regulations Rules and Regulations Home Laws on Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives National Firearms Act (NFA) NFA Handbook Background Checks for Responsible Persons Gun Control Act (GCA) Regulations for Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives ATF Launches New Era of Reform Federal Register Actions Rulings Open Letters Rules and Regulations Library Enhanced Regulatory Enforcement Policy Unified Agenda of Regulatory and Deregulatory Actions Definition of “Engaged in the Business” as a Dealer in Firearms Factoring Criteria for Firearms with Attached “Stabilizing Braces” Definition of Frame or Receiver Bump Stocks National Firearms Act Español The NFA was originally enacted in 1934. Similar to the current NFA, the original Act imposed a tax on the making and transfer of firearms defined by the Act, as well as a special (occupational) tax on persons and entities engaged in the business of importing, manufacturing, and dealing in NFA firearms. The law also required the registration of all NFA firearms with the Secretary of the Treasury. Firearms subject to the 1934 Act included shotguns and rifles having barrels less than 18 inches in length, certain firearms described as “any other weapons,” machine guns, and firearm mufflers and silencers. While the NFA was enacted by Congress as an exercise of its authority to tax, the NFA had an underlying purpose unrelated to revenue collection. As the legislative history of the law discloses, its underlying purpose was to curtail, if not prohibit, transactions in NFA firearms. Congress found these firearms to pose a significant crime problem because of their frequent use in crime, particularly the gangland crimes of that era such as the St. Valentine’s Day Massacre. The $200 making and transfer taxes on most NFA firearms were considered quite severe and adequate to carry out Congress’ purpose to discourage or eliminate transactions in these firearms. The $200 tax has not changed since 1934. As structured in 1934, the NFA imposed a duty on persons transferring NFA firearms, as well as mere possessors of unregistered firearms, to register them with the Secretary of the Treasury. If the possessor of an unregistered firearm applied to register the firearm as required by the NFA, the Treasury Department could supply information to State authorities about the registrant’s possession of the firearm. State authorities could then use the information to prosecute the person whose possession violated State laws. For these reasons, the Supreme Court in 1968 held in the Haynes case that a person prosecuted for possessing an unregistered NFA firearm had a valid defense to the prosecution — the registration requirement imposed on the possessor of an unregistered firearm violated the possessor’s privilege from self-incrimination under the Fifth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution. The Haynes decision made the 1934 Act virtually unenforceable. Title II of the Gun Control Act (GCA) of 1968 Title II amended the NFA to cure the constitutional flaw pointed out in Haynes. First, the requirement for possessors of unregistered firearms to register was removed. Indeed, under the amended law, there is no mechanism for a possessor to register an unregistered NFA firearm already possessed by the person. Second, a provision was added to the law prohibiting the use of any information from an NFA application or registration as evidence against the person in a criminal proceeding with respect to a violation of law occurring prior to or concurrently with the filing of the application or registration. In 1971, the Supreme Court reexamined the NFA in the Freed case and found that the 1968 amendments cured the constitutional defect in the original NFA. Title II also amended the NFA definitions of “firea
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly states that jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels. The changed text removes this jurisdiction clause entirely. The Perturbation Explanation states that the contract now lacks a clearly defined jurisdiction for dispute resolution, potentially conflicting with mandatory jurisdiction requirements for contracts under Austrian law. The Law Explanation states that Austrian law requires clarity regarding jurisdiction in contracts to ensure enforceability and that by deleting the jurisdiction clause, the enforceability of the contract under Austrian law becomes questionable. Although the provided scraped snippets do not directly address Austrian jurisdiction requirements, the removal of the explicitly stated jurisdiction directly contradicts the principle that contracts should clearly define jurisdiction, which the LLM law explanation states is a requirement under Austrian law.

perturbation.original_text: Jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels
perturbation.changed_text: Jurisdiction is agreed to be at Wels
perturbation.explanation: Moving the jurisdiction clause to the preamble, where it's less likely to be noticed and understood as a binding term, creates a structural flaw affecting enforceability. Preamble is normally introductory, while a jurisdiction clause needs to be a binding, operative section of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Austrian Law on Civil Procedure, jurisdiction clause requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian law requires clear and unambiguous jurisdiction clauses within the operative parts of a contract. By placing it in the preamble, it becomes less clear that both parties explicitly agreed to it as a legally binding term. Placing the clause in the preamble can be considered as mere introductory information. JN § 92 is the relevant Austrian regulation.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves a structural flaw where the jurisdiction clause is moved to the preamble. The Law Explanation states that Austrian law requires clear and unambiguous jurisdiction clauses within the operative parts of a contract, and placing it in the preamble makes it less clear that both parties explicitly agreed to it as a legally binding term. The absence of scraped law snippets doesn't invalidate this claim; the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation and the understanding of preambles as introductory, supports the determination that moving the jurisdiction clause creates a structural flaw that undermines its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Any amendments or additions to this agreement mus t be in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any amendments or additions to this agreement mus t be in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'amendments in writing' clause to the 'Subject matter of non-disclosure agreement' section, where it doesn't logically fit, undermines its enforceability. By moving it to a definition section, it can be misunderstood.
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian contract law (ABGB § 883) requires clarity and intent when it comes to contract amendments. Moving the clause concerning written amendments to the 'Subject matter' section makes it less prominent and may cause doubts about its actual binding nature compared to if it was placed in a section dedicated to 'Amendments' or 'General Terms'. The section is not related to amendments, thus the clause could be missed.
perturbation.location: Section 2. Subject matter of non-disclosure agreement

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the 'amendments in writing' clause to the 'Subject matter' section undermines its enforceability because it could be missed. The law explanation suggests that Austrian contract law (ABGB § 883) requires clarity and intent when it comes to contract amendments, and placement in an unrelated section reduces its prominence, potentially causing doubts about its binding nature. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing § 883 ABGB, discusses the form of contracts, stating that a contract can be oral or written. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit the placement of amendment clauses in other sections, the core concern raised by the explanation is the potential ambiguity introduced by relocating the clause, weakening its enforceability and violating the spirit of ensuring clear agreement on amendments, thus creating a structural flaw legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The DISCLOSEE agrees to keep any and all written, e lectronic and oral information and models conveyed or made available to it directly or indire ctly in the course of the collaboration strictly in confidence and not to disclose it or make it availa ble to any third party without the DISCLOSER's consent in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: The DISCLOSEE agrees to keep any and all written, e lectronic and oral information and models conveyed or made available to it directly or indire ctly in the course of the collaboration strictly in confidence and not to disclose it or make it availa ble to any third party without the DISCLOSER's consent in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the confidentiality clause to the 'signature block' section, where it appears as an afterthought, weakens its contractual force. This placement makes it seem less deliberate and binding than if it were in a dedicated 'Confidentiality' section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Austrian contract law (ABGB § 861) requires contractual terms to be clear and express the parties' intentions. Placing a core confidentiality clause in a signature area suggests it's secondary and not a primary binding agreement. Its presence in the signature block does not follow the principle that core obligations should be clearly specified within the body of the contract. It becomes buried and its purpose lost.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch – Wikipedia Zum Inhalt springen aus Wikipedia, der freien Enzyklopädie Der Titel dieses Artikels ist mehrdeutig. Zum liechtensteinischen Gesetz (ebenfalls ABGB abgekürzt) siehe Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (Liechtenstein) . Basisdaten Titel: Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch Langtitel: Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch für die gesammten deutschen Erbländer der Oesterreichischen Monarchie Abkürzung: ABGB Typ: Bundesgesetz Geltungsbereich: Republik Österreich Rechtsmaterie: Zivilrecht Fundstelle: JGS Nr. 946/1811 in ALEX Datum des Gesetzes: 1. Juni 1811 Inkrafttretensdatum: 1. Jänner 1812 Letzte Änderung: BGBl. I Nr. 33/2024 Gesetzestext: ABGB i.d.g.F. im RIS Bitte beachte den Hinweis zur geltenden Gesetzesfassung ! Titelblatt der ABGB-Ausgabe von 1811, ausgestellt im Heeresgeschichtlichen Museum in Wien Das Allgemeine bürgerliche Gesetzbuch ( ABGB ) ist die 1812 in den „deutschen Erbländern“ des Kaisertums Österreich in Kraft getretene und auch heute noch geltende wichtigste Kodifikation des Zivilrechts in Österreich und ist damit auch das älteste gültige Gesetzbuch des deutschen Rechtskreises . Es heißt „allgemein“, weil es für alle Personen in seinem Geltungsbereich, im Gegensatz zum gemeinen Recht , einheitlich und verbindlich gilt. „ Bürgerliches Recht “ bedeutet nach § 1 ABGB , dass das ABGB die „Privat-Rechte und Pflichten der Einwohner des Staates unter sich“ regelt. Geschichte [ Bearbeiten | Quelltext bearbeiten ] Entstehung [ Bearbeiten | Quelltext bearbeiten ] Die Vorarbeiten zu einer Kodifizierung des österreichischen Zivilrechts begannen bereits Mitte des 18. Jahrhunderts mit dem Codex Theresianus (1766) und dem darauf folgenden Entwurf Hortens (1774), die sich jeweils stark am Gemeinen Recht orientierten, [ 1 ] aber auch naturrechtliche Einflüsse enthielten. Mit Ausnahme des personenrechtlichen Teils des Entwurfs Horten, der am 1. Jänner 1787 als Josephinisches Gesetzbuch (1786 noch als Teil-ABGB ) in Kraft trat, erlangten sie allerdings keine Gesetzeskraft. Ende des 18. Jahrhunderts erarbeitete Karl Anton von Martini einen neuen Entwurf für eine Kodifizierung, der sich systematisch auf das Naturrecht gründete. [ 2 ] Dieser erlangte als Westgalizisches Gesetzbuch zunächst testweise in Westgalizien Geltung und als Ostgalizisches Gesetzbuch dann auch für Ostgalizien . [ 3 ] Mit Beginn des 19. Jahrhunderts wurde dieses als „Urentwurf“ [ 4 ] [ 5 ] von der Hofkommission für Gesetzsachen unter maßgeblicher Mitwirkung Franz von Zeillers , einem Schüler Martinis, im Auftrag Franz II. [ 6 ] überarbeitet und als ABGB per kaiserlichem Patent (Gesetz) am 1. Juni 1811 kundgemacht. Es trat mit 1. Jänner 1812 mit der Wirkung in Kraft, dass sämtliche Gesetze und Gewohnheitsrechte [ 7 ] mit Bezug auf das Allgemeine bürgerliche Recht außer Kraft gesetzt wurden. Entwicklung des Geltungsbereiches [ Bearbeiten | Quelltext bearbeiten ] Der Geltungsbereich des ABGB umfasste die gesamten Deutschen Erbländer der Österreichischen Monarchie , worunter all jene Länder verstanden wurden, die nicht zur ungarischen Krone gehörten ( Cisleithanien ). [ 8 ] Ab 1853 wurde es als Folge der Reichsverfassung 1849 und der anschließenden Verfassungsgrundsätze (RGBl. 4/1852 Nr. 33 der Beilage) [ 9 ] auch in Kroatien-Slawonien , Ungarn , im Temescher Banat , in der serbischen Vojvodina (RGBl. 246/1852) [ 10 ] und in Siebenbürgen (RGBl. 99/1853) [ 11 ] in Kraft gesetzt, [ 12 ] in Ungarn hingegen bereits 1861 als Folge des Oktoberdiploms und der Autonomiebestrebungen Ungarns wieder außer Kraft gesetzt (Képviselőházi irományok, 1861, I. Kötet, 11. sz. [ 13 ] ). [ 14 ] Der Zerfall der Österreichisch-Ungarischen Monarchie hatte keine sofortigen Auswirkungen auf den Geltungsbereich des ABGB. Es bestand in den Nachfolgestaaten, etwa in Österreich (StGBl 140/1920), [ 15 ] das nun auch aus dem Burgenland bestand, in Teilen der Tschechoslowakei ( Nr. 11/1918 Sb. ), Polens, [ 16 ] [ 17 ] Rumäniens, [ 18 ] und im Königrei
perturbation.justification: The altered contract's placement of the confidentiality clause in the signature block weakens its contractual force, creating a structural flaw that contradicts Austrian contract law (ABGB § 861), as explained by the LLM. The LLM states that ABGB § 861 requires contractual terms to be clear and express the parties’ intentions. Placing a core confidentiality clause in a signature area suggests it's secondary. The changed contract arguably buries a core obligation, diminishing its clarity and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The supplier (supplier = contractor) assures that a ny and all necessary rights for the production and distribution of the furnished and delivered materia ls and products are available and holds Fronius harmless from all and any claims by third parties r esulting from an infringement of industrial and /or intellectual property rights/patents.
perturbation.changed_text: The supplier (supplier = contractor) assures that a ny and all necessary rights for the production and distribution of the furnished and delivered materia ls and products are available and holds Fronius harmless from all and any claims by third parties r esulting from an infringement of industrial and /or intellectual property rights/patents.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Indemnification' clause to the 'Definitions' Section makes it less noticeable and enforceable. Usually indemnification is placed in its own section, or in the dispute resolution section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Austrian law ABGB § 1311, for indemnification to be fully enforceable, it must be clear that both parties knowingly and willingly accepted the provision. By placing such a provision in the 'Definitions' section, it undermines the concept that it was fully and explicitly agreed upon by the parties. Definitions can often be glossed over. By moving this, it makes the clause less impactful and its placement is misleading.
perturbation.location: Section 2. Subject matter of non-disclosure agreement
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the indemnification clause to the 'Definitions' section makes it less noticeable and enforceable, which is an example of a structural flaw. The law explanation states that, under Austrian law ABGB § 1311, for indemnification to be fully enforceable, it must be clear that both parties knowingly and willingly accepted the provision. The law explanation also posits that definitions can often be glossed over, which makes the clause less impactful and its placement misleading. The placement of the indemnification clause is misleading and less impactful, thus undermining the concept that it was fully and explicitly agreed upon by the parties, creating a contradiction. The scraped snippets were not helpful.
